includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Pump prices haven't gotten the memo that crude cooled — record $128/bbl gap vs Brent
items.idea: Global retail fuel prices have unwound only ~45% of their early-2026 spike while Brent has given back ~70% of its rally, leaving the fuel-retail-vs-Brent gap at a record $128/bbl on a 28-day moving average. The culprit is tight refining capacity and wide wholesale margins, not crude itself — refinery outages across Russia and the Middle East are still removing usable barrels from the product market even as crude risk premium fades.
items.angle: This is the number that keeps the 'inflation is cooling' narrative from landing with consumers — central banks can point at Brent all they want, drivers only see the pump.
items.tweetA: Brent gave back 70% of its 2026 spike. Pump prices gave back 45%. Retail margins pocketed the other 25 points — that's the disinflation story central banks keep promising and can't deliver at the register.
items.tweetB: Refiners are running the same play airlines ran post-COVID: quietly rebuild margin while everyone's staring at the headline commodity. A $128/bbl retail-Brent gap is the toll booth nobody's pricing in.

items.title: Alibaba's AI business: $1.3B revenue vs a $55B infra bill, and its own engineers are quitting over the pivot
items.idea: Alibaba's AI products generated $1.3B in Q1 revenue — under 4% of total revenue — against a pledge to spend over $55B on AI infrastructure through 2027. Qwen became the world's most-downloaded open-source AI system (about 1M downloads/day at its peak), but the push to monetize has fractured the team: lead engineer Lin Junyang left in March along with several colleagues over disagreements on commercializing Qwen, and Alibaba shipped three proprietary (non-open) models in April alone.
items.angle: Alibaba is the live test case for whether open-source AI leadership converts into revenue — the answer so far is a resounding no, and Meta's parallel pivot from open to paid (see Muse Spark item) suggests this isn't a China-only problem.
items.tweetA: Alibaba is spending $55B on AI infra through 2027. Its AI products did $1.3B in revenue last quarter — annualized that's under 10% of the capex bill. Qwen's lead engineer already quit over the strategy fight. This is the open-source hangover nobody priced in.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching whether OpenAI and Anthropic can monetize. Alibaba just showed you what happens when you give the model away for free THEN try to flip the switch: your own team quits rather than build the paywall.

items.title: Anthropic told two US Senators that Alibaba ran 24,000 fake accounts to steal its models
items.idea: Per NYT reporting cited in the newsletter, Anthropic sent a letter to Senators Tim Scott and Elizabeth Warren accusing Alibaba of 'brazenly' and 'illicitly' trying to copy its technology using 24,000 fraudulent accounts to harvest data from its AI systems. The Pentagon separately placed Alibaba on a blacklist of firms it says support the Chinese military; Alibaba disputes both the IP claim and the blacklist designation.
items.angle: This is a concrete, quantified escalation in the AI-IP-theft narrative — 24,000 accounts is a specific, checkable number, not vague hand-waving about 'China copies everything,' and it's now sitting in a formal Congressional letter.
items.tweetA: Anthropic told Congress Alibaba ran 24,000 fake accounts to scrape its models. That's not a lawsuit footnote — that's a frontier AI lab formally accusing a $180B+ tech giant of industrial-scale IP theft, in writing, to sitting Senators.
items.tweetB: The 'China copies everything' narrative usually stays vague. 24,000 fraudulent accounts is a number, not a vibe. Watch whether this becomes the next flashpoint for export controls, not just a PR spat.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Meta prices Muse Spark 1.1 at ~25% of frontier rivals — the AI race just became a margin war
items.idea: Meta launched its first serious paid developer API with Muse Spark 1.1, pricing it at roughly 25% of comparable OpenAI/Anthropic frontier models, per Zuckerberg. Goldman has floated agentic AI driving monthly token usage into the quadrillions by 2030 — but cheap near-frontier intelligence means the capex-payback story for every other lab just got harder to underwrite.
items.angle: Meta can subsidize AI with ad-engine cash; OpenAI and Anthropic can't. A price war Meta starts and can uniquely afford to lose is really an attrition campaign against everyone else's balance sheet.
items.tweetA: Meta just priced Muse Spark 1.1 at ~25% of OpenAI/Anthropic. Goldman says agentic AI could push monthly tokens into the quadrillions by 2030. Put those together and the AI trade shifts from 'best model' to 'who can sell intelligence at bandwidth prices and survive.'
items.tweetB: Meta can subsidize AI with ad dollars. OpenAI and Anthropic can't. A price war only one competitor can afford to lose isn't competition, it's attrition against everyone else's cap table.

items.title: Six sell-side desks all assume Hormuz 'normalizes' within 60 days — while the US and Iran are still bombing each other
items.idea: Goldman, Morgan Stanley, HSBC and JPM all independently assume Strait of Hormuz flows normalize within 60 days and price a 2027 oil surplus. But the ~150M bbls that drained since June 17 (~8mb/d) were mostly stranded floating storage leaving, not new production — actual production shut-in still sits near 6-6.6mb/d by Goldman's own numbers, and the US is escorting tankers through the Oman lane with AIS transponders switched off.
items.angle: Wilson draws the direct parallel to April 2020, when sell-side consensus insisted on permanent demand destruction right before production shut-ins became the entire story a month later — six analysts converging on the same wrong prior isn't confirmation, it's correlated error.
items.tweetA: Goldman, Morgan Stanley, HSBC and JPM all independently assume Hormuz normalizes within 60 days — while the US and Iran are still actively bombing each other and tankers are running with AIS dark. That's not six analysts agreeing, that's six anchored to the same wrong prior.
items.tweetB: The 150M bbls that 'proved' the oil glut thesis were stranded floating storage draining out, not new supply. Shut-in production is still ~6.6mb/d missing. Same mistake as April 2020 — everyone modeled demand destruction and missed the supply story until it hit them in the face.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — 6 charts referenced (Morgan Stanley: Crude Oil Exports, MEG transits in/out; Goldman: Flows out of SOH, Production shut-in, Imbalance), paywalled in email — worth opening on Substack.

items.title: Malala Yousafzai turned activism into an investment fund — Recess Capital, for women's sports
items.idea: Malala Yousafzai and her husband Asser Malik partnered with McKinsey to build Recess Capital, described as a mission-driven investment platform aimed at boosting opportunities for female athletes globally — a Nobel Peace Prize laureate moving from advocacy into being an actual capital allocator.
items.angle: The women's sports funding wave isn't just WNBA/NWSL valuations popping — it's activists-turned-allocators building purpose-built capital pools, which is a stickier, less sentiment-driven source of demand than a hot franchise sale.
items.tweetA: Malala Yousafzai isn't just an activist anymore — she and her husband built an actual investment vehicle, Recess Capital, to fund women's sports. Nobel Peace Prize winner turned fund GP. Women's sports as an institutional asset class keeps getting more real.
items.tweetB: The interesting trend isn't another women's sports valuation headline — it's advocates converting moral authority directly into allocator status. That's a different, stickier kind of capital than a private-equity flip.

items.title: McKinsey: employees are ready for agentic AI, leaders aren't — the readiness gap is inverted from every past tech cycle
items.idea: McKinsey's 'three horizons' framework for AI transformation argues employees are ready to embrace agentic AI while many leaders acknowledge they are not yet prepared to organize around it — closing that readiness gap, not employee reluctance, is framed as the actual bottleneck to unlocking value.
items.angle: In every prior enterprise-tech cycle (cloud, mobile, even early internet), management chased hype while the workforce dragged its feet. If the gap has genuinely inverted this time, the investable signal shifts from 'who has the best AI vendor' to 'whose leadership actually gets out of the way.'
items.tweetA: McKinsey's line: employees are ready for agentic AI, leaders aren't. That's backwards from every prior tech cycle, where management chased the hype and workers dragged their feet. This time the bottleneck is at the top.
items.tweetB: If the workforce is ahead of management on AI adoption, the alpha isn't picking the best AI vendor — it's picking the companies whose leadership actually gets out of the way and lets the horizon-3 stuff happen.

items.title: Ceasefire fully collapsed: CENTCOM hits ~90 more Iranian targets, Iran strikes US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain
items.idea: A second wave of CENTCOM strikes hit roughly 90 Iranian military targets; Iran retaliated with drone and missile strikes on US facilities in Kuwait and Bahrain. At the NATO summit in Ankara, Trump said the ceasefire/MOU 'is over, as far as I'm concerned' and called further talks 'a waste of time,' claiming Iran has violated the agreement 'every day.' Despite this, markets were 'notably calmer' than mid-week, with Nexo describing it as 'a contained flare-up rather than a return to full-scale disruption.'
items.angle: Two strikes deep into a collapsed ceasefire and equities/crypto recovered same-day — either markets have correctly priced a contained regional conflict, or they're one Hormuz-closure headline away from a violent repricing.
items.tweetA: Trump just told NATO the Iran ceasefire is 'over, as far as I'm concerned' — after CENTCOM hit a SECOND wave of ~90 targets and Iran struck US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain. Brent only round-tripped back to $77.50. The market has decided this is theater, not war. Someone's wrong.
items.tweetB: Two strikes deep into a collapsed ceasefire and risk assets recovered the same day. Either markets have correctly priced a contained regional war, or they're one Hormuz-closure headline from a very violent repricing.

items.title: Brent spiked 8% mid-week to $78.80, then faded to $77.50 as the market called it 'contained'
items.idea: WTI jumped ~5% above $74 and Brent hit ~$78.80 (a third straight up session after an ~8% mid-week spike) before easing back toward $77.50 as traders judged the escalation a contained flare-up rather than a supply-disrupting event — even as Hormuz traffic slowed to a near-halt and both sides raised the prospect of a full closure.
items.angle: HFIR's Wilson (same window, different newsletter) argues this 'contained' read is insane given shut-in production is still ~6.6mb/d short — two credible analysts looking at the same tape reaching opposite conclusions is exactly the kind of divergence worth having a position on.
items.tweetA: Brent spiked 8% mid-week to $78.80 on the strikes, then faded back to $77.50 by Thursday — the market pricing 'contained flare-up.' HFIR's Wilson, same week, calls that read insane given shut-in production is still 6.6mb/d short. Two good analysts, opposite conclusions. Pick a side.
items.tweetB: Oil is now trading the war like a headline-risk event instead of a physical supply-balance problem. That gap between price action and actual barrels missing from the market is exactly where the next violent move lives.

items.title: FOMC Minutes show a Fed split 9-8 — less hawkish than feared, but oil alone pushed July-hike odds from <20% to >27%
items.idea: The FOMC Minutes revealed the Fed split 9 to 8 on the 2026 rate path, seen as less hawkish than markets feared, with growing inflation concern offset by slightly receding labor-market worries. Yet swap traders still pushed July-hike odds above 27% (from under 20% the prior week) purely on the oil spike, with the next hike pulled forward to October from December.
items.angle: The Fed didn't even need to say anything hawkish — crude did the tightening for them. That decouples the next rate move from anything the FOMC controls and hands it to whatever happens in the Strait of Hormuz.
items.tweetA: Fed's 9-8 split on the 2026 path is basically a coin flip dressed up as a committee decision. But July-hike odds still jumped from under 20% to over 27% purely off the oil spike — the Fed didn't even need to say anything hawkish, crude did it for them.
items.tweetB: Everyone was braced for hawkish Minutes and got a non-event — that's the real dovish surprise here. The actual driver of the next hike isn't the Fed's words anymore, it's whatever Iran does to the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Gold fell 4 straight days into an active war. Bitcoin held $63K, 9% above June's close, through the same war
items.idea: While gold fell for a fourth consecutive day toward $4,060 during the Iran escalation, Bitcoin recovered from a sub-$62K dip back to ~$63,000, sitting 9% above June's monthly close. CoinDesk frames this as Bitcoin now tracking front-end Treasury yields more closely than crude or gold — a 'rotation from gold into bitcoin as a rates-sensitive asset' if $60K continues to hold.
items.angle: This is the first real out-of-sample test of 'digital gold': an actual shooting war, and gold sold off while Bitcoin held. If that pattern repeats through the next escalation, the reserve-hedge narrative starts migrating from gold to BTC in real allocator behavior, not just Twitter takes.
items.tweetA: Gold fell four straight days into an active shooting war. Bitcoin held $63K, 9% above June's close, through the SAME war. If gold is the traditional geopolitical hedge and BTC just did that job better, the 'digital gold' thesis got its first real out-of-sample test — and gold lost this round.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin now tracks front-end Treasury yields more closely than it tracks crude or gold. That's not a crypto story, that's a rates story wearing a crypto ticker. Position accordingly, not on the war headlines.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — no embedded chart, but BTC-vs-gold divergence since the ceasefire collapse (July 6-9) is a ready-made screenshot: plot BTC price vs XAU over the same 4 days.

items.title: BTC exchange supply lowest since 2017, ETH lowest since 2015 — and open interest is falling, not rising
items.idea: Bitcoin's exchange supply hit roughly 6.6% of circulating supply, its lowest since 2017; Ethereum's exchange supply hit its lowest since 2015. Both readings have historically preceded multi-quarter bull phases. Meanwhile open interest declined from 272K to 266K BTC over the same period — meaning the move is happening on coins leaving exchanges, not on fresh leverage.
items.angle: Declining OI alongside a multi-year supply-squeeze low is the combination that separates a structural setup from a leveraged froth rally — worth distinguishing from the usual 'number go up on high OI' pattern that tends to unwind violently.
items.tweetA: BTC exchange supply is at its lowest since 2017 (6.6% of float). ETH's is lowest since 2015. Both readings have historically preceded multi-quarter bull phases — and open interest is DECLINING at the same time, meaning this isn't leveraged froth, it's actual coins leaving exchanges.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching ETF flows as the institutional tell. The quieter, more structural signal is the supply side: there's simply less BTC and ETH sitting anywhere it could be sold, and that number keeps shrinking.

items.title: SpaceX hits a new post-IPO low the same week Blue Origin closes a $10B raise at $130B
items.idea: SpaceX (SPCX) fell to a new post-IPO low near $145, just 8% above its $135 IPO price and down from a $225 record. In the same window, Blue Origin is reportedly nearing a $10B raise at a $130B valuation, led by Coatue's $4B with Bezos personally adding $2B.
items.angle: Private capital is more bullish on space right now than public markets are — that valuation spread between a public stock trading near its IPO price and a private round pricing at a record valuation rarely stays open for long.
items.tweetA: SpaceX just hit a new post-IPO low, only 8% above its IPO price and down 36% from its $225 peak. Same week, Blue Origin is closing a $10B raise at a $130B valuation with Coatue leading. Private capital is more bullish on space than public markets right now — that spread rarely stays open.
items.tweetB: $SPCX trading near its IPO price while Bezos personally adds another $2B into Blue Origin tells you retail's public-market enthusiasm for the space trade cooled faster than the smart money's conviction did.

items.title: Real-world-asset perps now trade 1.5x spot volume — leverage arrived before adoption
items.idea: Real-world-asset perpetual futures did $26.4B in monthly volume versus $17.7B in spot RWA trading, with TradFi-style perps generating roughly 8x spot-RWA volume across 2026 so far. Binance led with roughly $500B in volume, a 36% share.
items.angle: The RWA pitch was 'tokenize real assets, bring TradFi on-chain' — what actually showed up first is leverage layered on top of the tokenized assets, not organic ownership demand. Financialization is outrunning adoption, same as every prior crypto cycle.
items.tweetA: Real-world-asset perpetuals did $26.4B in monthly volume vs $17.7B in spot — derivatives are now 1.5x spot in an asset class that's supposed to be about owning the underlying thing. Binance alone is 36% of that. TradFi speculation just found its way onto crypto rails before the actual assets did.
items.tweetB: The RWA narrative was sold as 'bring real assets on-chain.' What showed up first was leverage on top of the tokenized assets. Financialization always outruns adoption — RWA is just the latest category to prove it.

items.title: The CLARITY Act draft could hit the Senate floor this month
items.idea: The CLARITY Act draft — the first serious attempt at a statutory US crypto market-structure framework — could arrive as soon as next week and reach a Senate vote later this month, though it still faces hurdles. This lands alongside a wave of other structural crypto news: Robinhood's Arbitrum-built L2 topped $100M TVL a week after launch, and the SEC's 'Regulation Crypto' agenda is slated for July.
items.angle: Everyone's trading the Hormuz headlines this week. The regulatory catalyst that could actually resolve years of US crypto market-structure uncertainty is sitting quietly in the Senate queue for later this month.
items.tweetA: The CLARITY Act draft could hit the Senate floor this month. If it passes, it's the first real statutory crypto market-structure framework in the US — landing right as BTC is decoupling from risk assets. Timing matters more than the headline suggests.
items.tweetB: Everyone's trading the Hormuz headlines. The regulatory catalyst that actually resolves years of crypto market-structure uncertainty is quietly sitting in the Senate queue for later this month.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: G10 FX carry is back — and for the first time in years, spot isn't fighting it
items.idea: Carry-to-vol in crosses like USD/CAD and EUR/CHF is unusually rich because rate dispersion across G10 central banks remains wide while FX volatility has stayed remarkably contained. Unlike the old regime where carry was just equity beta in a costume (clipped in risk-on, wiped out in risk-off), some carry expressions are now trading closer to risk-neutral or even defensive. JPY and CHF remain the cleanest funding legs, though both carry tripwires — BOJ intervention risk and a renewed gold rally complicating the SNB's terms-of-trade channel, respectively.
items.angle: When carry stops behaving like disguised equity beta, it changes portfolio math — you can earn the coupon with less direct exposure to an equity drawdown, which is a meaningfully different risk profile than the post-GFC carry trade most people still have priced in their heads.
items.tweetA: Carry-to-vol in USD/CAD and EUR/CHF is 'unusually rich' right now because rate dispersion is wide AND vol is quiet — a combination that almost never lasts. When it breaks, watch the funding currencies first: JPY and CHF.
items.tweetB: The old carry trade was just equity beta in a costume — clip the coupon until risk-off wipes it out. This cycle's version is trading closer to risk-neutral, which means when it unwinds, equities won't be the thing that tells you first.

items.title: 20%+ of all blockchain transactions fail to execute — on Solana, non-vote failures top 40%
items.idea: Tiger Research's report on institutional on-chain data infrastructure states that depending on network congestion, more than 20% of all blockchain transactions fail to execute, and on Solana specifically the failure rate among non-vote transactions can exceed 40%. If a payment system mistakes one of these failed transactions for a successful one, it can reject a legitimate follow-up payment over a balance mismatch that shouldn't exist.
items.angle: This is the base-rate reliability number nobody quotes when pitching AI-agent micropayments (see the Drip/x402 item elsewhere this window) — put an autonomous agent in charge of decisions on the fastest, cheapest chain, and it's making them against data that's wrong more than 4 times in 10.
items.tweetA: More than 20% of all blockchain transactions fail to execute. On Solana, the failure rate for non-vote transactions tops 40%. Now put AI agents in charge of autonomous payments on that rail with no human checking the balance first. That's not a hypothetical risk, that's the base rate.
items.tweetB: The agentic-payments narrative assumes the chain just works. Nobody's pricing in that on the fastest, cheapest chain, nearly half of non-vote transactions don't even land — and that's before you ask an AI to make a financial decision based on that data.

charts.desc: Sponsored research report — charts likely in full PDF

items.title: CME launches single stock futures on July 27 — overnight risk transfer comes to names like Oracle
items.idea: CME Group is launching single stock futures on individual names (including Oracle) on July 27, giving the market 24-hour overnight risk transfer on single stocks for the first time. The framework piece notes this will change how hedging flows move the underlying stocks, with dislocations increasingly trading around the clock rather than gapping at the cash open.
items.angle: Every time an exchange adds overnight risk transfer to a new asset class, realized opening-gap risk tends to fall while intraday/overnight vol rises — single names are about to get the treatment index futures already have.
items.tweetA: CME launches single stock futures on July 27 — Oracle and others get 24-hour overnight risk transfer for the first time. That's plumbing, not a headline, but it means single-name dislocations start trading around the clock instead of gapping at the open.
items.tweetB: Every time an exchange adds overnight risk transfer to a new asset class, gap risk at the open goes down and round-the-clock vol goes up. Single stocks are about to get the index-futures treatment on July 27.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Gold's break below $4,050 was a positioning flush, not the end of the buying — central banks are still stacking at 2x the old pace
items.idea: Gold's drop into the low $4,020s looked like a stop-loss cascade among crowded late longs rather than a fundamental regime shift. Central banks have been accumulating gold at close to 1,000 tonnes a year over the past four years, roughly double the ~500-tonne annual pace of the prior decade. The latest reserve-manager survey shows a record share expecting their own institution's gold reserves to rise over the next 12 months, and a large majority expecting the dollar's share of global reserves to be moderately or significantly lower over five years.
items.angle: Two different clocks: the trader's clock says respect the technical damage and don't chase; the reserve clock says central banks are still buying, slowly and heavily, regardless of this week's stop-loss cascade. Don't confuse a liquidation flush with a change in the structural bid.
items.tweetA: Central banks are buying gold at ~1,000 tonnes a year now, double the ~500t/yr pace of the prior decade. This week's drop to $4,020 wasn't that buyer stepping back — it was leveraged longs getting flushed. Two totally different clocks, and only one of them just blinked.
items.tweetB: A record share of reserve managers expect their OWN gold holdings to rise this year, and most expect the dollar's reserve share to shrink over five years. That's not a trade, that's a decade-long reallocation happening quietly underneath every 'gold crashes' headline.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — no embedded chart, but the central-bank buying pace (1,000t/yr vs 500t/yr prior decade) is a strong standalone bar chart; pull from World Gold Council survey data referenced in the piece.

items.title: Drip wants AI agents to pay writers per-article via x402, settled in USDC
items.idea: Drip, co-founded by Justin Blau and Michael Blau, lets AI agents pay independent publishers for paywalled content on a per-use basis, built on the x402 and MPP agentic payment standards with USDC settlement on Base and Tempo. The founders chose financial analysis as their launch niche.
items.angle: Micropayments for content have failed as a business model for two decades running human friction as the excuse. The pitch this time is that the payer isn't a human anymore — it's a bot that doesn't feel friction and doesn't abandon a checkout flow.
items.tweetA: Drip wants AI agents to pay writers per-article via x402, settled in USDC on Base and Tempo. Micropayments have failed as a business model for 20 years straight — the new pitch is that the payer isn't human anymore, so the usual friction excuse doesn't apply.
items.tweetB: If this works, it inverts the paywall model completely: instead of humans subscribing to dodge the wall, AI agents pay per-scrape and humans get answers for free through the agent. Publishers become B2B data vendors to bots, not subscription businesses for people.

items.title: Japan's 10-year yield just hit a near 3-decade high
items.idea: Japan's 10-year JGB yield pushed to its highest level in nearly 30 years, described in the digest as a global-liquidity headwind that crypto markets absorbed without much visible damage this week.
items.angle: Everyone's watching the Fed's 9-8 split. The more dangerous split might be in Tokyo — a multi-decade high in JGB yields is the one macro variable that could unwind every yen-funded G10 carry position (see the FX carry item above) at once.
items.tweetA: Japan's 10-year yield just hit a near 3-decade high, at the same time the yen is the most obvious carry-funding currency risk in FX. JGB yields ripping higher is the one macro variable that could blow up every G10 carry trade simultaneously.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the Fed's 9-8 split this week. The more dangerous split might be in Tokyo, where multi-decade-high JGB yields are quietly pulling the rug out from under every yen-funded position in the world.
